hook: Grassroots-saved 1913 bathhouse, once East Asia's largest public bath
facts: Built 1913 as the Beitou Public Bathhouse, modelled on Japanese spa resorts
Reportedly the largest public bath in East Asia at the time
Abandoned, then rediscovered in 1994 by local schoolchildren and teachers
Reopened 1998 as Taiwan's first museum saved by a community petition
w: Schoolchildren on a 1994 field trip found this derelict Japanese bathhouse, raised 1913 as supposedly the largest public bath in East Asia, and petitioned to save it. Their campaign worked, making it Taiwan's first museum rescued by ordinary citizens. Stained glass, a vast Roman tub, tatami upstairs. Civic pride, soaked in sulfur.

hook: Steaming jade-green sulfur pool that locals dubbed Hell Valley
facts: Source spring for much of Beitou's hot-spring water, near boiling
Rare green sulfur waters; once one of the colonial 'Eight Views of Taiwan'
Type locality of hokutolite, a radioactive mineral named for Beitou
Long fenced off after past scaldings; viewing now from a boardwalk
legend: Locals call it Hell Valley for the sulfurous steam that boils up year-round
w: Locals named it Hell Valley, and the milky jade pool earns it: water near boiling, sulfur steam drifting over the rocks all year. A geologist found a faintly radioactive mineral here and named it hokutolite, after Beitou. Egg-boilers once scalded themselves, so now you watch from a boardwalk. A landscape that smells of brimstone.

hook: Rare surviving Japanese-era Zen temple built by railway workers
facts: Built 1905, rebuilt 1934, in Japanese Rinzai Zen (Myoshinji) style
Funded largely by employees of the colonial Governor-General's Railway Bureau
One of very few intact Japanese Buddhist temples left in Taiwan
Listed as a Taipei historic site in 1998
w: Up a quiet lane above the hot-spring valley sits a wooden Zen hall that railway clerks paid for in 1934. Rinzai lines, deep eaves, hinoki beams: among the last intact Japanese Buddhist temples in Taiwan after most were razed post-1945. A bodhisattva of safe travel watches over it. Faith built on timetables.

hook: Taiwan's first certified green building, a timber library in the park
facts: Opened 2006; first structure in Taiwan to earn green-building certification
Solar roof, deep balconies and wooden louvres cut heat and power use
Collection specialises in ecology and conservation themes
Stands inside Beitou Park amid the hot-spring trees
w: A timber-and-glass ark moored among the park's old trees, opened 2006 as the first green-certified building in Taiwan. Solar panels on a light roof, deep balconies and wooden louvres do the cooling that air-con usually would. Rainwater feeds the toilets; the shelves lean hard into ecology. Reading among the leaves, by design.

hook: Hillside villa where a famous cursive calligrapher spent his last years
facts: Built late 1930s as a fine Japanese-era residential compound
Later home of Yu Youren, renowned master of cursive 'grass' script
Named for the plum trees the calligrapher favoured
Now a small museum and exhibition space open to the public
w: One of Beitou's smartest Japanese-era villas, two floors of pale render on the slope, became the late retreat of Yu Youren, the great master of cursive grass script. He loved plum blossom, and the garden took its name from his trees. He came here in his seventies to write and withdraw. A calligrapher's hideaway, ink still in the air.

n: Believe It or Not Curiosity Museum
hook: Cabinet-of-curiosities oddment hall off Tamsui Old Street
facts: Tucked along the lanes near Tamsui Old Street and Chongjian Street
Displays freak specimens: a four-legged chicken, a tiny human skull, oversized rodents
Mixes preserved oddities with a few live snakes and scorpions
An old-school sideshow museum in the Ripley tradition
w: Off the snack-stall crush of Tamsui Old Street hides a proper sideshow cabinet: a four-legged chicken, a skull the size of a walnut, jarred specimens of things that shouldn't be. A python and some scorpions hold court live. Pure Ripley-era spectacle, gloriously unbothered by good taste. Bring a strong stomach and a fiver.

hook: Red-walled Dutch fort the locals called the Red-Hair Castle
facts: Spanish first built here in 1628; rebuilt by the Dutch as Fort Antonio after 1642
British leased it as a consulate from 1868 and painted the walls red
Adjacent consular residence added in 1891 in colonial brick
Held by Britain into the 1970s before returning to Taiwan
legend: Locals dubbed it the Red-Hair Castle, after the 'red-hair' foreigners who held it
w: Spanish, then Dutch, then British: this blunt fortress over the estuary changed flags for three centuries. Locals nicknamed it the Red-Hair Castle after the foreigners who held it, and the British made the name literal, painting the walls oxblood. A Victorian consular house sits beside it. Empire in one stubborn cube.

hook: Whitewashed colonial customs villa with arches over the river mouth
facts: Built around 1870 as a residence for Qing-era maritime customs officers
Spanish-colonial style: white render, deep verandah, eleven arches
Frames a sweeping view of the Tamsui River estuary
Restored and listed as a New Taipei historic building
w: Eleven whitewashed arches and a deep colonnade, raised around 1870 for the foreign officers who ran the Qing customs house when Tamsui boomed on tea and camphor. From the verandah the river slides out to the sea, the same view that taxed every cargo passing. Sea-trade money set in plaster. They call it the Little White House.

hook: Mackay's 1882 college, walls bound with rice, lime and sugar
facts: Founded 1882 by Canadian missionary George Leslie Mackay
Named Oxford College to thank donors in Oxford County, Ontario
Walls made from steamed glutinous rice, lime and sugar-cane juice
Among Taiwan's oldest higher-education buildings; now a museum
w: Mackay, the bearded Canadian who pulled Tamsui's rotten teeth and built its schools, raised this college in 1882 and named it for the Ontario county that funded it. The walls hold together on steamed glutinous rice, lime and sugar-cane juice, brick from Amoy. One of Taiwan's first colleges. Sweet mortar, hard learning.

hook: Taiwan's only intact Hakka temple to the Dingguang Buddha
facts: Built 1822 by Hakka migrants from Dingzhou, Guangdong
Enshrines the Dingguang Buddha, a guardian worshipped almost only by Hakka
Reputed the only fully preserved Hakka temple of its kind in Taiwan
Hidden in lanes a short walk from Tamsui Old Street
w: Hakka migrants from Dingzhou built this two-hall temple in 1822 and brought their own guardian, the Dingguang Buddha, scarcely worshipped elsewhere in Taiwan. While the Hokkien filled Tamsui with Mazu shrines, the Hakka kept their saint here. Said to be the only fully intact Hakka temple left. A minority memory in stone.

hook: Qing coastal fort that never fired a shot, near-perfectly intact
facts: Built 1886 by Liu Mingchuan after the Sino-French War
Designed on Western fortification principles by a German engineer
Never saw combat, so the earthworks and gun emplacements survive intact
Gate inscription reads 'the northern gate locks the key'
w: Stung by the French raid on Tamsui, Governor Liu Mingchuan threw up this Western-style gun fort in 1886, hired a German to design it, and carved over the gate that the northern gate locks the key. Then the guns never fired in anger, so the ramparts and emplacements stand almost as built. A fortress preserved by the peace it threatened.

hook: Qing-to-Showa shophouse street frozen mid-century in Monga
facts: Streetscape dates to the early Qing era in old Monga (Wanhua)
Name traced to the hide-stripping or bark-stripping trades once worked here
Architecture layers Qing, Japanese and Republican shopfronts
Restored and opened 2010 as a heritage and education centre
w: A whole block of Monga shophouses caught mid-century: Qing arcades, Japanese tilework and Republican shopfronts standing shoulder to shoulder. The odd name points back to a hide- or bark-stripping trade once worked along here. New roads bypassed it into decay, which is exactly why it survived. The film Monga shot here. Old Taipei, paused.

hook: Veterans' illegal hillside slum reborn as a stacked artist warren
facts: Begun late 1940s by Kuomintang veterans as an anti-aircraft post and squat
Grew into a dense illegal settlement above the Xindian River
Slated for demolition for decades, then preserved after protests
Reopened 2010 as an artist village; some original families still live there
w: Mainland veterans dug an anti-aircraft post into this riverside hill after 1949, then built homes around it without a permit. The city tried to bulldoze the maze for thirty years; protesters saved it. Now studios thread the same crooked stairs, with a few original families still there. A squatters labyrinth, kept on purpose.

hook: Tiny Dihua Street temple where singles court the Old Man of the Moon
facts: Built 1859 by migrants from Tong'an, Fujian; still run by one family
Crams over 600 deities into roughly 150 square metres
Famed for Yue Lao, the matchmaker god of love and marriage
Devotees leave offerings of sweets and red thread to seek a partner
legend: They say Yue Lao ties an invisible red thread between destined lovers
w: Barely 150 square metres on old Dihua Street, yet it packs over six hundred deities, supposedly Taiwan's densest. One family has tended it since 1859. The draw sits in a corner: Yue Lao, the Old Man of the Moon, who they say ties a red thread between destined lovers. Hopefuls leave sweets and beg a match. Love, retail-dense.

hook: UNESCO-honoured medicine-god temple of the Tong'an clan
facts: Begun 1804 by Tong'an migrants, replacing a 1742 wooden shrine
Dedicated to Baosheng Dadi, the Taoist god of medicine
Won a UNESCO Asia-Pacific heritage conservation award
Its name means to protect those of Tong'an
legend: Baosheng Dadi, said to have once cured a tiger and a dragon, draws the sick to pray
w: Settlers from Tong'an raised this temple from 1804 to their hometown healer, Baosheng Dadi, god of medicine, and named it to protect their own. They say the deity once cured a dragon and a tiger. The carvings so impressed judges it won a UNESCO conservation award. A clan's hospital chapel, gilded to the rafters.

hook: Low-slung arsenal workers' village marooned under the skyscrapers
facts: Built from 1948 for staff of the 44th Arsenal and their families
Taipei's first juancun, a military dependents' village
A 1999 fire hastened evacuation and a fight over its future
Saved and reopened 2003 as a cultural park beneath Taipei 101
w: When the 44th Arsenal workers shipped over in 1948, they were parked in barracks meant to be temporary that lasted half a century. Taipei's first dependents village, low tiled roofs huddled together. A 1999 fire nearly finished it; protest saved it. Now it crouches in the shadow of Taipei 101, the old century eyeballing the new.

hook: Eighty-metre tunnel-grotto of deities bored through a hillside
facts: Guandu Temple founded 1712, dedicated to the sea-goddess Mazu
A roughly 80-metre rock grotto was bored through the hill in the 1970s
Lined with 28 guardian devas leading to a thousand-armed Guanyin
Tunnel exits onto a balcony over the Tamsui River
legend: A symbolic mortar at the cave mouth is said to suppress all evil
w: Behind one of Taipei's oldest Mazu temples, founded 1712, someone in the 1970s drilled a corridor straight through the hill. Eighty metres of grotto, twenty-eight glowering guardian devas, a thousand-armed Guanyin at the dark end. A mortar at the mouth supposedly grinds away all evil. Step out onto the river. Devotion as tunnelling.

hook: The last Qing government office in Taiwan, moved into a garden
facts: Built 1888 to lodge imperial envoys to the new Taiwan province
The only surviving Qing-dynasty yamen office building in Taiwan
Dismantled and relocated into the Taipei Botanical Garden in 1933
Stands among the garden's ponds and old trees as a museum
w: Raised in 1888 to house mandarins inspecting the brand-new province, this timber hall counts as the only Qing government office still standing in Taiwan. The Japanese took it apart mid-modernisation and rebuilt it in the Botanical Garden in 1933. Now it sits among lotus ponds, an emperor's outpost adrift in a greenhouse city.

hook: Taipei's oldest street, wall to wall with herbal medicine houses
facts: Centre of the city's tea, fabric and medicine trades since the 1850s
Lined with century-old Chinese herbal-medicine shops and dried-goods stores
Tea once made up roughly 90 percent of Taiwan's exports through here
Baroque and Minnan shophouse facades survive along the lane
w: Taipei's oldest trading street still smells of it: cabinets of dried roots, deer antler, mushrooms and bark, weighed out by pharmacists whose families have stood here since the tea boom. Baroque and Minnan shopfronts line the lane. Lunar New Year turns it to a crush. Commerce as apothecary, three centuries deep.

hook: Church-mission campus and dreamy backdrop of Jay Chou's Secret
facts: Founded 1914 as a Presbyterian school in the Mackay mission tradition
Octagonal-tower hall blends Chinese, Japanese and Western design
Jay Chou's alma mater and the main location of his 2007 film Secret
Usually closed to the public, opening only at limited times
w: An old Presbyterian school from 1914, its octagonal-towered hall fusing pagoda eaves with Western brick, sits behind walls that usually stay shut. Jay Chou studied here, then came back to film Secret in its corridors, and now fans make pilgrimages to a piano romance that never quite happened. Mission school turned movie set.
